title: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis
authors: William M. Lee, Jules L. Dienstag

items.title: Why DILI is a Leading Cause of Acute Liver Failure
items.content: Drug-induced liver injury (DILI) is the most common cause of acute liver failure among patients. It often arises from unrecognized exposure to various chemicals, including prescribed drugs, over-the-counter medications, and herbal or dietary supplements.
keyPoints: DILI is the most common cause of acute liver failure in patients.
Unrecognized exposure includes work-related chemicals, prescription drugs, OTC medications, and CAMs.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · Toxic and Drug-Induced Hepatitis

items.title: How Drugs Cause Liver Injury: Mechanisms and Pathways
items.content: Hepatotoxic drugs can directly injure hepatocytes through free radicals or metabolic intermediates causing lipid peroxidation. They may also activate immune responses, stimulate apoptosis, or disrupt bile excretion pathways. Secondary injuries from these disruptions can lead to hepatocyte necrosis, cholestasis, and lactic acidosis.
keyPoints: Direct injury via free-radical or metabolic intermediate leading to membrane lipid peroxidation.
Activation of immune system components, apoptotic pathways, or bile excretion disruption.
Secondary injuries: hepatocyte necrosis, cholestasis, lactic acidosis, and microvesicular steatosis.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · Toxic and Drug-Induced Hepatitis

items.subtopic: DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)
items.title: Mechanisms of Drug-Induced Liver Injury
items.content: Drug-induced liver injury can occur through various mechanisms, including disruption of intracellular calcium homeostasis, covalent binding of drugs to cytochrome P450 enzymes, and activation of apoptotic pathways. These processes can lead to hepatocyte damage or death.
keyPoints: Disruption of intracellular calcium homeostasis leads to cell membrane rupture and lysis (mechanism A).
Covalent binding of drugs to cytochrome P450 enzymes creates nonfunctioning adducts (mechanism C).
Activation of apoptotic pathways by TNF-α or Fas triggers programmed cell death (mechanism E).
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)
items.title: Characteristics of Direct Toxic Hepatitis
items.content: Direct toxic hepatitis is dose-dependent, occurs predictably in exposed individuals, and has a short latent period. It results from systemic poisons or liver-converted toxic metabolites, leading to characteristic morphologic abnormalities.
keyPoints: Occurs with predictable regularity and is dose-dependent.
Latent period between exposure and injury is usually several hours, with clinical manifestations delayed for 24–48 h.
Examples include carbon tetrachloride causing centrilobular zonal necrosis and Amanita phalloides producing massive hepatic necrosis at ~10 mg.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)
items.title: Idiosyncratic Drug Reactions and Hepatotoxicity
items.content: Idiosyncratic drug reactions are infrequent, unpredictable, and not clearly dose-dependent. They may occur days to weeks after exposure and can involve mild, transient serum aminotransferase elevations or hypersensitivity manifestations.
keyPoints: Occurrence is infrequent (1 in 10^3-10^3 patients) and unpredictable.
Liver injury typically occurs between 5 and 90 days post-exposure.
Mild, transient serum aminotransferase elevations may resolve with continued drug use.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Drug-Induced Hepatotoxicity and Autoimmune Features
items.content: Certain drugs can cause hepatotoxicity through mechanisms involving HLA haplotypes or cytokine activation. Some drugs, like nitrofurantoin, minocycline, hydralazine, and α-methyldopa, are associated with autoimmune hepatitis features, including positive antinuclear antibodies (ANAs) and autoantibodies such as anti-LKM2.
keyPoints: HLA haplotypes linked to hepatotoxicity for drugs like amoxicillin-clavulanate and flucloxacillin
Autoimmune hepatitis phenotype with high likelihood of positive ANAs: nitrofurantoin, minocycline, hydralazine, α-methyldopa
Anti-LKM2 antibodies directed against cytochrome P450 enzyme
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 2)

items.title: Differentiating Hepatocellular and Cholestatic Injury via R Value
items.content: The R value, the ratio of ALT to alkaline phosphatase values expressed as multiples of their upper limits of normal, helps distinguish between hepatocellular and cholestatic injury. An R value >5.0 indicates hepatocellular injury, <2.0 suggests cholestatic injury, and a value between 2.0 and 5.0 implies mixed injury.
keyPoints: R value >5.0: hepatocellular injury
R value <2.0: cholestatic injury
R value 2.0-5.0: mixed hepatocellular-cholestatic injury
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 2)

items.title: Recognizing Patterns of Drug-Induced Cholestasis
items.content: Drug-induced cholestasis can vary from mild to severe, with patterns ranging from bland cholestasis to sclerosing cholangitis and ductopenic cholestasis. The severity is influenced by the drug's interaction with canalicular membrane transporters or genetic defects in these proteins.
keyPoints: Bland cholestasis: estrogens, 17α-substituted androgens
Inflammatory cholestasis: amoxicillin-clavulanic acid, oxacillin, erythromycin estolate
Sclerosing cholangitis: floxuridine post-hepatic metastases treatment
Ductopenic cholestasis: carbamazepine, levofloxacin
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Hy's Law in Drug-Induced Hepatotoxicity
items.content: Hy's Law, named after Dr. Hyman Zimmerman, is a critical predictor of severe hepatotoxicity during drug trials. It states that if jaundice occurs during a phase 3 trial, there is a high likelihood of serious liver injury, with a 10:1 ratio between cases of jaundice and acute liver failure.
keyPoints: Jaundice in clinical trials indicates potential severe hepatotoxicity.
Hy's Law predicts a 10:1 ratio between jaundice and liver failure.
Troglitazone case highlights the importance of postmarketing surveillance.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 3)

items.title: Challenges in Establishing Drug-Induced Liver Injury (DILI) Causality
items.content: Establishing causality for DILI is challenging due to its presumptive nature and the similarity of its clinicopathologic picture with other disorders. Multiple assessment variables are needed, including temporal association, clinical-biochemical features, type of injury, extrahepatic features, past records of the agent, and exclusion of other causes.
keyPoints: DILI is a presumptive diagnosis requiring multiple supportive assessments.
Temporal association, clinical-biochemical features, and type of injury are crucial for causality assessment.
RUCAM and RECAM are methods used to assess causality but have limitations.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 3)

items.title: Postmarketing Surveillance in Identifying Toxic Drugs
items.content: Postmarketing surveillance is essential for identifying toxic drugs after they are introduced to the market. The case of troglitazone, which led to liver failure post-approval, underscores the importance of ongoing monitoring and evaluation.
keyPoints: Troglitazone's withdrawal highlights the need for vigilant postmarketing surveillance.
No fully approved drugs have been withdrawn since 2001 due to improved safety measures.
Black box warnings are used to indicate caution with certain medications.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 3)

items.title: Management of Acetaminophen Hepatotoxicity
items.content: N-acetylcysteine is the effective treatment for acetaminophen hepatotoxicity. In cases of drug-induced liver injury (DILI), acute liver failure occurs in 10% of patients, with spontaneous recovery in less than 30%. Liver transplantation is required for over 40% of those who reach severe levels of acute liver failure.
keyPoints: N-acetylcysteine is effective specifically for acetaminophen hepatotoxicity.
Acute liver failure develops in 10% of DILI cases.
<30% spontaneous recovery once acute liver failure threshold is reached.
>40% require liver transplantation at severe levels of acute liver failure.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · Toxic and Drug-Induced Hepatic Disease

items.title: Withdrawal and Monitoring in DILI
items.content: Immediate withdrawal of the suspected agent is crucial when aminotransferase levels reach five times the upper limit of normal or at the first sign of an adverse reaction. Continued use despite liver injury symptoms can lead to lethal outcomes.
keyPoints: Withdrawal indicated when aminotransferases are 5x ULN or with initial adverse reactions.
Continued agent use in face of liver injury signs may result in fatal outcomes.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · Toxic and Drug-Induced Hepatic Disease

items.title: Recognizing Acetaminophen Hepatotoxicity
items.content: Acetaminophen is the leading cause of acute liver failure in the Western world. Clinical suspicion should be raised by extremely high aminotransferase levels with low bilirubin, even if overdose history is unclear.
keyPoints: Up to 72% of Scandinavian patients progress to encephalopathy and coagulopathy.
A single dose of 10–15 g can cause liver injury; ≥25 g often leads to fatal fulminant disease.
Blood levels >300 μg/mL at 4 hours predict severe damage; <150 μg/mL suggest unlikely hepatic injury.
High aminotransferase levels with low bilirubin are characteristic of acetaminophen hepatotoxicity.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 1)

items.title: Timing and Severity of Acetaminophen Hepatotoxicity
items.content: Symptoms and liver damage from acetaminophen toxicity have a distinct timeline, with early symptoms appearing within hours and maximal hepatic injury occurring days later.
keyPoints: Early symptoms (nausea, vomiting) occur 4–12 h post-ingestion.
Hepatic injury becomes apparent 24–48 h after ingestion.
Maximal abnormalities and failure are evident 3–5 days post-ingestion.
Aminotransferase levels can exceed 10,000 IU/L.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 1)

items.title: Mechanism and Risk Factors of Acetaminophen Hepatotoxicity
items.content: Acetaminophen is metabolized to a hepatotoxic metabolite, NAPQI, which can cause liver damage when glutathione is depleted. Various factors can potentiate this toxicity.
keyPoints: NAPQI is detoxified by binding to glutathione; depletion leads to hepatocyte necrosis.
Risk factors include alcohol use, phenobarbital, INH, starvation, and conditions reducing hepatic glutathione levels.
Alcohol induces CYP2E1, increasing NAPQI production.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 1)

items.title: Acetaminophen Toxicity in Chronic Alcoholics and FDA Recommendations
items.content: Chronic alcoholics are at increased risk of acetaminophen toxicity even at standard doses due to suppressed hepatic glutathione production. The FDA has recommended reducing the daily dose from 4 g to 3 g, with further reductions for those with chronic alcohol use.
keyPoints: Toxic dose in chronic alcoholics may be as low as 2 g.
FDA recommends reducing daily acetaminophen dose to 3 g.
Acetaminophen-containing products should have prominent labeling about liver injury risk.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 2) | Acetaminophen Overdosage

items.title: Management of Acetaminophen Overdose and Use of N-acetylcysteine
items.content: Treatment for acetaminophen overdose includes gastric lavage, activated charcoal, and N-acetylcysteine. N-acetylcysteine is most effective when administered within 8 hours but can still be beneficial up to 24-36 hours post-ingestion.
keyPoints: N-acetylcysteine reduces hepatic necrosis severity if given early (within 8 h).
Loading dose of N-acetylcysteine: 140 mg/kg over 1 hour, followed by 70 mg/kg every 4 hours for 15–20 doses.
Plasma acetaminophen levels >200 µg/mL at 4 h or >100 µg/mL at 8 h indicate high-risk hepatotoxicity.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 2) | Acetaminophen Overdosage

items.title: Indications for Liver Transplantation in Acute Acetaminophen Hepatotoxicity
items.content: Liver transplantation may be necessary if hepatic failure signs persist despite N-acetylcysteine therapy. Early arterial blood lactate levels >3.5 mmol/L can predict the need for liver transplantation.
keyPoints: Signs of hepatic failure include progressive jaundice, coagulopathy, confusion.
Early arterial blood lactate levels >3.5 mmol/L indicate a high likelihood of needing liver transplantation.
Acute renal injury occurs in ~75% of severe cases but is usually self-limited.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 2) | Acetaminophen Overdosage

items.title: Recognizing Mild vs. Severe INH Hepatotoxicity
items.content: INH is a key antituberculous agent but can cause hepatotoxicity, which varies in severity. Initial mild elevations of serum aminotransferase levels (<200 IU/L) are common and often self-limited, allowing continuation if no progressive symptoms occur. Severe hepatocellular injury may develop with latency up to 6 months, particularly in alcoholics or those on certain medications.
keyPoints: 10% of patients experience elevated serum aminotransferase levels during the first few weeks of INH therapy.
Mild elevations are typically <200 IU/L and often self-limited.
Severe hepatocellular injury can occur with latency up to 6 months, especially in alcoholics or those on barbiturates, rifampin, or pyrazinamide.
Severe hepatic injury may lead to encephalopathy, potentially requiring liver transplantation.
Hepatotoxicity risk increases substantially after age 35 and is highest over age 50.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ISONIAZID HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.title: Monitoring and Managing INH Hepatotoxicity
items.content: Careful monitoring of aminotransferase levels is recommended, especially in older patients. Monthly monitoring may be questioned; however, patient education on symptom awareness (nausea, fatigue, jaundice) can prevent fatalities from continued use despite illness. The incidence of severe toxicity may decline with better management and less frequent INH use.
keyPoints: Monthly aminotransferase level monitoring is common but debated in efficacy.
Patient education on symptom awareness (nausea, fatigue, jaundice) can prevent fatalities.
Severe hepatotoxicity occurs in ~2% of patients >50 years old under careful monitoring.
Incidence of severe INH toxicity may be declining due to better management and less frequent use.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ISONIAZID HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.subtopic: SODIUM VALPROATE HEPATOTOXICITY (TOXIC AND IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION) | NITROFURANTOIN HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)
items.title: Recognize and Manage Sodium Valproate Hepatotoxicity
items.content: Sodium valproate, an anticonvulsant, can cause severe hepatic toxicity, especially in children. While asymptomatic elevations of serum aminotransferase levels are common (up to 45%), major hepatotoxicity is rare but serious when it occurs. The metabolite 4-pentenoic acid may be the culprit for liver injury, and patients with mitochondrial enzyme deficiencies are at higher risk. Valproate toxicity can be linked to specific HLA haplotypes and mitochondrial DNA mutations.
keyPoints: Sodium valproate is associated with severe hepatic toxicity, particularly in children.
Asymptomatic elevations of serum aminotransferase levels occur in up to 45% of treated patients.
Major hepatotoxicity is rare but can manifest as jaundice, encephalopathy, and hepatic failure.
4-pentenoic acid, a metabolite of valproate, may cause liver injury.
Risk factors include mitochondrial enzyme deficiencies, HLA haplotypes (DR4 and B*1502), and mutations in mitochondrial DNA polymerase gamma 1.
IV administration of carnitine may ameliorate toxicity.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · SODIUM VALPROATE HEPATOTOXICITY (TOXIC AND IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION) | NITROFURANTOIN HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.subtopic: SODIUM VALPROATE HEPATOTOXICITY (TOXIC AND IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION) | NITROFURANTOIN HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)
items.title: Identify and Address Nitrofurantoin Hepatotoxicity
items.content: Nitrofurantoin, used for urinary tract infections, can cause acute or chronic hepatitis, often indistinguishable from autoimmune hepatitis. The drug's toxicity may have a prolonged latency period due to its intermittent use, particularly in recurrent cystitis cases. Autoantibodies are common and may require immunosuppressive treatment if the condition persists. Additionally, interstitial pulmonary fibrosis is a possible complication.
keyPoints: Nitrofurantoin can cause acute or chronic hepatitis, resembling autoimmune hepatitis.
Toxicity may have a prolonged latency period due to intermittent use.
Autoantibodies to nuclear components, smooth muscle, and mitochondria are observed.
Immunosuppressive medication may be necessary for treatment.
Interstitial pulmonary fibrosis can occur with nitrofurantoin use.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · SODIUM VALPROATE HEPATOTOXICITY (TOXIC AND IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION) | NITROFURANTOIN HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.title: Recognize and Monitor for Hepatotoxicity with Amoxicillin-Clavulanate
items.content: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is a common cause of drug-induced liver injury (DILI) in the U.S. and Europe, often presenting as cholestatic or mixed hepatotoxicity. Symptoms include nausea, anorexia, fatigue, jaundice, and pruritus, with potential for prolonged effects post-discontinuation.
keyPoints: Most common DILI agent in the U.S. and Europe
Symptoms: nausea, anorexia, fatigue, jaundice, pruritus
Rare rash occurrence
Potential for vanishing bile duct syndrome
Hepatotoxicity may manifest after drug withdrawal
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · AMOXICILLIN-CLAVULANATE HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC MIXED REACTION) | AMIODARONE HEPATOTOXICITY (TOXIC AND IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.title: Identify and Manage Amiodarone-Induced Liver Injury
items.content: Amiodarone therapy can lead to serum aminotransferase elevations in 15–50% of patients, with clinically significant liver disease developing in less than 5%. It may cause pseudoalcoholic liver injury, detectable by phospholipid-laden lysosomal bodies. Hepatic granulomas and acute idiosyncratic hepatocellular injury are rare.
keyPoints: 15–50% of patients experience aminotransferase elevations
<5% develop clinically significant liver disease
Features: phospholipidosis, interference with drug metabolism
Pseudoalcoholic liver injury mimics alcoholic hepatitis
Liver injury may persist months after discontinuation
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · AMOXICILLIN-CLAVULANATE HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC MIXED REACTION) | AMIODARONE HEPATOTOXICITY (TOXIC AND IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.title: Recognizing Cholestatic Liver Injury from Anabolic Steroids
items.content: Anabolic steroids, often used by bodybuilders and sold as diet supplements, can cause profound cholestasis. This is characterized by jaundice with a cholestatic laboratory profile rather than hepatic. The injury may take weeks to months to resolve.
keyPoints: Cholestasis without substantial inflammation or necrosis in liver tissue
Serum aminotransferase levels usually <100 IU/L
Moderately elevated serum alkaline phosphatase levels
Bilirubin levels frequently exceeding 342 μmol/L (20 mg/dL)
Prolonged jaundice can last more than 10 weeks
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ANABOLIC STEROIDS (CHOLESTATIC REACTION) | TRIMETHOPRIM-SULFAMETHOXAZOLE HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.title: Understanding Hepatotoxicity from Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole
items.content: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, used for urinary tract infections and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, can cause hepatotoxicity with a uniform latency period of several weeks. It often presents with eosinophilia and rash, resembling DRESS syndrome.
keyPoints: Acute hepatocellular necrosis is common, but cholestatic features are frequent
Hepatotoxicity attributed to the sulfamethoxazole component
Increased risk in persons with HIV infection
Unique HLA associations identified in European Americans and African Americans
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ANABOLIC STEROIDS (CHOLESTATIC REACTION) | TRIMETHOPRIM-SULFAMETHOXAZOLE HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.subtopic: HMG-COA REDUCTASE INHIBITORS (STATINS) (IDIOSYNCRATIC MIXED HEPATOCELLULAR AND CHOLESTATIC REACTION)
items.title: Statins and Liver Enzyme Monitoring: When to Continue or Discontinue
items.content: In patients taking statins, asymptomatic elevations in aminotransferase activity occur in 1-2%. Minor elevations are common initially but usually transient. Statin hepatotoxicity is rare and does not increase with underlying liver diseases like chronic hepatitis C or hepatic steatosis.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic, reversible elevations of aminotransferase activity occur in 1-2% of patients on statins.
Minor aminotransferase elevations are common during the first weeks but are usually transitory.
Clinically meaningful aminotransferase elevations are rare post-statin use and do not differ from placebo recipients.
Liver test monitoring is generally unnecessary for patients on statins unless significant abnormalities persist.
Statin therapy can continue in asymptomatic isolated aminotransferase elevation cases.
Statins are safe even with chronic hepatitis C, hepatic steatosis, or other liver diseases.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · HMG-COA REDUCTASE INHIBITORS (STATINS) (IDIOSYNCRATIC MIXED HEPATOCELLULAR AND CHOLESTATIC REACTION) | ALTERNATIVE AND COMPLEMENTARY MEDICINES (IDIOSYNCRATIC HEPATITIS, STEATOSIS)

items.subtopic: ALTERNATIVE AND COMPLEMENTARY MEDICINES (IDIOSYNCRATIC HEPATITIS, STEATOSIS)
items.title: Risks of Alternative Medicines in Hepatotoxicity
items.content: Herbal and dietary supplements account for over 20% of drug-induced liver injury (DILI) cases. These products often lack regulatory oversight and can cause acute hepatitis-like lesions or other liver damage, especially when combined with substances that affect cytochrome P450 enzymes.
keyPoints: >20% of DILI in the U.S. is due to herbal medications lacking scientific efficacy proof and safety oversight.
Commonly implicated products include weight loss agents like Hydroxycut and Herbalife.
Jin Bu Huan use can lead to acute hepatitis-like lesions such as focal hepatocellular necrosis and microvesicular steatosis.
Pyrrolizidine alkaloids in some herbal preparations can cause veno-occlusive disease.
Cytochrome P450 enzyme interactions may enhance or amplify the toxicity of alternative medicines.
Herbal products may contain undisclosed active chemicals, such as NSAIDs like diclofenac.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · HMG-COA REDUCTASE INHIBITORS (STATINS) (IDIOSYNCRATIC MIXED HEPATOCELLULAR AND CHOLESTATIC REACTION) | ALTERNATIVE AND COMPLEMENTARY MEDICINES (IDIOSYNCRATIC HEPATITIS, STEATOSIS)

items.subtopic: CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND OTHER IMMUNOTHERAPIES FOR CANCER | HIGHLY ACTIVE ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY FOR HIV INFECTION (MITOCHONDRIAL TOXIC, IDIOSYNCRATIC, STEATOSIS; HEPATOCELLULAR, CHOLESTATIC, AND MIXED)
items.title: Immune-Related Hepatotoxicity from Checkpoint Inhibitors
items.content: Checkpoint inhibitors like ipilimumab (CTLA-4), pembrolizumab (PD-1), and nivolumab can cause hepatotoxicity characterized by hepatitis with positive ANAs. This condition responds to glucocorticoid therapy, and liver histology shows nonspecific acute hepatic injury mediated by CD8+ lymphocytes.
keyPoints: Checkpoint inhibitors include ipilimumab (CTLA-4), pembrolizumab (PD-1), nivolumab (PD-1).
Hepatotoxicity manifests as hepatitis with positive ANAs within weeks of treatment initiation.
Liver histology shows nonspecific acute hepatic injury, not resembling chronic autoimmune hepatitis.
Condition responds to glucocorticoid therapy and can often be managed by temporarily halting then restarting the immunotherapy.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND OTHER IMMUNOTHERAPIES FOR CANCER | HIGHLY ACTIVE ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY FOR HIV INFECTION (MITOCHONDRIAL TOXIC, IDIOSYNCRATIC, STEATOSIS; HEPATOCELLULAR, CHOLESTATIC, AND MIXED)

items.subtopic: CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND OTHER IMMUNOTHERAPIES FOR CANCER | HIGHLY ACTIVE ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY FOR HIV INFECTION (MITOCHONDRIAL TOXIC, IDIOSYNCRATIC, STEATOSIS; HEPATOCELLULAR, CHOLESTATIC, AND MIXED)
items.title: HAART-Associated Hepatotoxicity in HIV Patients
items.content: In HIV patients, HAART can cause hepatotoxicity, often complicated by co-infections like hepatitis B or C. Key agents include zidovudine, didanosine, stavudine, ritonavir, indinavir, amprenavir, nevirapine, and efavirenz. Co-management of chronic viral hepatitis is crucial as HAART can affect liver injury progression.
keyPoints: HAART hepatotoxicity was common in early HIV therapy but less frequent now.
Agents implicated include zidovudine, didanosine, stavudine (nucleoside analogues), ritonavir, indinavir, amprenavir, nevirapine, efavirenz.
Co-infection with hepatitis B or C complicates the management of HAART hepatotoxicity.
HAART can affect chronic viral hepatitis progression and liver injury; e.g., immunologic reconstitution may cause liver-cell injury in hepatitis B co-infected patients.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND OTHER IMMUNOTHERAPIES FOR CANCER | HIGHLY ACTIVE ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY FOR HIV INFECTION (MITOCHONDRIAL TOXIC, IDIOSYNCRATIC, STEATOSIS; HEPATOCELLULAR, CHOLESTATIC, AND MIXED)

items.question: Which mechanism is NOT mentioned as a way that hepatotoxic drugs can cause liver injury?
options: Direct injury to the hepatocyte via free-radical formation.
Activation of components of the innate or adaptive immune system.
items.explanation: The passage mentions interference with bile canalicular pumps leading to accumulation, not inhibition of bile acid production.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · Toxic and Drug-Induced Hepatitis

items.question: What is the most common cause of acute liver failure according to the passage?
items.explanation: The passage states that drug-induced liver injury is the most common cause of acute liver failure.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · Toxic and Drug-Induced Hepatitis

items.question: Which receptors are mentioned as being involved in the induction of drug hepatotoxicity?
items.explanation: The passage mentions the constitutive androstane receptor (CAR) as being involved in drug hepatotoxicity.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · Toxic and Drug-Induced Hepatitis

items.subtopic: DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)
items.question: Which phase of drug metabolism involves the conversion of drugs to water-soluble forms for excretion?
items.explanation: Phase II reactions, such as glucuronidation and sulfation, convert drugs to water-soluble forms for excretion.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary cause of most drug hepatotoxicity?
options: Formation of a phase I toxic metabolite
items.explanation: Most drug hepatotoxicity results from the formation of a phase I toxic metabolite.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)
items.question: Which type of chemical hepatotoxicity is dose-dependent and occurs with predictable regularity?
items.explanation: Direct toxic hepatitis is dose-dependent and occurs predictably in exposed individuals.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)
items.question: What can contribute to drug hepatotoxicity by preventing the inactivation of harmful compounds?
items.explanation: Glutathione depletion can contribute to hepatotoxicity by preventing the inactivation of harmful compounds.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)

items.question: Which drug is most frequently implicated among cases of DILI for causing inflammatory cholestasis?
items.explanation: Amoxicillin-clavulanic acid is the most frequently implicated antibiotic among cases of DILI for causing inflammatory cholestasis.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 2)

items.question: What type of injury does an R value >5.0 indicate in drug-induced liver injury?
items.explanation: An R value >5.0 is associated with hepatocellular injury.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 2)

items.question: Which drug-induced liver condition resembles chronic rejection following liver transplantation?
items.explanation: Ductopenic cholestasis or vanishing bile duct syndrome resembles chronic rejection following liver transplantation.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following drugs is associated with a phenotype of autoimmune hepatitis and positive antinuclear antibodies (ANAs)?
items.explanation: Nitrofurantoin is associated with a phenotype of autoimmune hepatitis and positive antinuclear antibodies (ANAs).
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 2)

items.question: What is 'Hy's Law' and who was it named after?
options: 'Hy's Law' indicates that the occurrence of jaundice in a phase 3 trial predicts severe hepatotoxicity, named after Dr. Hyman Zimmerman.
'Hy's Law' states that liver failure occurs at a 10:1 ratio to cases of jaundice during drug trials, named after Dr. John Smith.
'Hy's Law' suggests that any elevation in aminotransferases is a predictor of severe hepatotoxicity, named after Dr. Hyman Zimmerman.
'Hy's Law' indicates that liver injury is unlikely if jaundice occurs during a trial, named after Dr. Hyman Zimmerman.
items.explanation: Hy's Law predicts severe hepatotoxicity when jaundice occurs in phase 3 trials and was named after Dr. Hyman Zimmerman.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 3)

items.question: What significant event occurred with troglitazone post-marketing?
options: Troglitazone was found to be safe and continued in the market.
Troglitazone cases of jaundice were recognized during phase 3 trials, leading to its withdrawal.
Troglitazone caused severe hepatotoxicity only after it was withdrawn from the market.
Troglitazone was replaced by rosiglitazone and pioglitazone due to their similar safety profiles.
items.explanation: Jaundice cases during phase 3 trials led to troglitazone's withdrawal post-marketing.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 3)

items.question: Which method is currently used by the U.S. Drug-Induced Liver Injury Network for assessing causality?
items.explanation: The U.S. Drug-Induced Liver Injury Network uses a structured expert opinion process for causality assessment.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 3)

items.question: What is the significance of aminotransferase elevations in drug trials?
options: They are definitive indicators of severe liver failure.
They are used as possible surrogates for more serious toxicity.
They indicate that the drug is safe and effective.
items.explanation: Aminotransferase elevations may serve as surrogates for more serious toxicity in drug trials.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 3)

items.question: Which treatment is specifically effective for acetaminophen hepatotoxicity?
items.explanation: N-acetylcysteine is effective for acetaminophen hepatotoxicity.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · Toxic and Drug-Induced Hepatic Disease

items.question: What percentage of patients with DILI develop acute liver failure?
items.explanation: Acute liver failure develops in 10% of patients with DILI.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · Toxic and Drug-Induced Hepatic Disease

items.question: Which agent is used for mushroom poisoning but has questionable value?
items.explanation: Silibinin is used for mushroom poisoning but of questionable value.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · Toxic and Drug-Induced Hepatic Disease

items.question: Which blood level of acetaminophen, measured 4 hours after ingestion, is predictive of the development of severe hepatic injury?
items.explanation: Levels >300 μg/mL 4 hours after ingestion predict severe hepatic injury.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary metabolic pathway for acetaminophen that leads to its detoxification?
options: Phase I reaction via cytochrome P450 CYP2E1
Phase II reaction to sulfate and glucuronide metabolites
items.explanation: Acetaminophen is predominantly detoxified by phase II reactions forming sulfate and glucuronide metabolites.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 1)

items.question: What condition may potentiate hepatic injury from acetaminophen due to reduced glutathione levels?
items.explanation: Starvation can reduce hepatic glutathione levels, potentiating acetaminophen-induced injury.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of N-acetylcysteine in acetaminophen hepatotoxicity?
options: It increases cytochrome P450 CYP2E1 activity
It promotes renal excretion of acetaminophen
items.explanation: N-acetylcysteine acts as an antidote by replenishing depleted glutathione.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 1)

items.question: In chronic alcoholics, what is the potentially toxic dose of acetaminophen?
items.explanation: In chronic alcoholics, the toxic dose of acetaminophen may be as low as 2 g.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 2) | Acetaminophen Overdosage

items.question: What is the recommended action if acetaminophen plasma levels are high after ingestion?
items.explanation: High acetaminophen blood levels require the administration of N-acetylcysteine to reduce hepatic necrosis.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 2) | Acetaminophen Overdosage

items.question: What is the limit for the acetaminophen component in opioid combination products?
items.explanation: The limit for the acetaminophen component in opioid combination products has been lowered to 325 mg per tablet.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 2) | Acetaminophen Overdosage

items.question: When should N-acetylcysteine therapy be ideally started after acetaminophen ingestion?
items.explanation: N-acetylcysteine therapy should be begun within 8 hours of acetaminophen ingestion.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 2) | Acetaminophen Overdosage

items.question: Which patient group is more frequently affected by acute hepatocellular DILI secondary to INH?
options: Alcoholics and patients taking certain other medications
Patients with a positive tuberculin skin test
Patients monitored monthly for aminotransferase levels
items.explanation: Acute hepatocellular DILI secondary to INH is more frequent in alcoholics and patients taking certain other medications.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ISONIAZID HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.question: What is the typical latency period for acute hepatocellular DILI secondary to INH?
items.explanation: Acute hepatocellular DILI secondary to INH has a variable latency period up to 6 months.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ISONIAZID HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.question: What is the approximate incidence of hepatotoxicity in patients over age 50 who are carefully monitored during INH therapy?
items.explanation: Even for patients >50 years of age monitored carefully, hepatotoxicity occurs in only ~2%.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ISONIAZID HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.assertion: Drug-induced liver injury is the most common cause of acute liver failure.
items.reason: DILI requires careful history-taking to identify unrecognized exposure to chemicals used in work or at home, drugs taken by prescription or bought over the counter, and herbal or dietary supplement medicines.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why DILI is the most common cause of acute liver failure.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · Toxic and Drug-Induced Hepatitis

items.subtopic: DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)
items.assertion: Acetaminophen is an example of a direct toxin.
items.reason: Direct toxins result in predictable and characteristic morphologic abnormalities.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)
items.reason: These reactions are dose-dependent and predictable.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; idiosyncratic reactions are infrequent. The reason is also false.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · DRUG METABOLISM | LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 1)

items.assertion: High-dose chemotherapeutic agents can lead to venocclusive disease.
items.reason: This occurs due to injury of sinusoidal lining cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 2)

items.assertion: Oral contraceptives produce necrosis or fatty change.
items.reason: This is due to their estrogenic and progestational compounds.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; oral contraceptives do not typically produce necrosis or fatty change.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 2)

items.assertion: The occurrence of jaundice during phase 3 trials is a predictor of liver failure.
items.reason: 'Hy's Law' states that there is a 10:1 ratio between cases of jaundice and liver failure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 3)

items.assertion: Troglitazone was withdrawn from the market due to post-marketing liver failure cases.
items.reason: No instances of liver failure were recognized during phase 3 trials.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; liver failure cases were not recognized until after marketing.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · LIVER INJURY CAUSED BY DRUGS (part 3)

items.assertion: Methotrexate, tamoxifen, and amiodarone have been implicated in the development of cirrhosis.
items.reason: These drugs can lead to chronic liver injury resulting in cirrhosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · Toxic and Drug-Induced Hepatic Disease

items.assertion: Acetaminophen hepatotoxicity can be diagnosed by measuring serum acetaminophen-Cys adducts.
items.reason: These adducts are formed when NAPQI binds to hepatocyte macromolecules due to glutathione depletion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 1)

items.assertion: Alcohol consumption has no impact on acetaminophen metabolism.
items.reason: Alcohol induces cytochrome P450 CYP2E1, increasing the production of NAPQI.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; alcohol does affect acetaminophen metabolism by inducing CYP2E1.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 1)

items.assertion: N-acetylcysteine reduces the severity of hepatic necrosis in acetaminophen overdose.
items.reason: It provides sulfhydryl donor groups to replete glutathione, which detoxifies toxic metabolites.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 2) | Acetaminophen Overdosage

items.assertion: Routine use of N-acetylcysteine has increased the occurrence of fatal acetaminophen hepatotoxicity.
items.reason: N-acetylcysteine is a sulfhydryl donor that helps detoxify toxic metabolites.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; routine use of N-acetylcysteine has reduced fatal acetaminophen hepatotoxicity.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ACETAMINOPHEN HEPATOTOXICITY (DIRECT TOXIN) (part 2) | Acetaminophen Overdosage

items.assertion: The incidence of severe INH toxicity may be declining.
items.reason: This is due to less frequent use and/or better management of the drug.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the decline in severe INH toxicity.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · ISONIAZID HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.subtopic: SODIUM VALPROATE HEPATOTOXICITY (TOXIC AND IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION) | NITROFURANTOIN HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)
items.assertion: Nitrofurantoin hepatotoxicity may have a latency period longer than 6 months.
items.reason: This is due to its intermittent, recurrent use for treatment of conditions like cystitis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · SODIUM VALPROATE HEPATOTOXICITY (TOXIC AND IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION) | NITROFURANTOIN HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.subtopic: AMOXICILLIN-CLAVULANATE HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC MIXED REACTION) | AMIODARONE HEPATOTOXICITY (TOXIC AND IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)
items.assertion: Amiodarone can cause pseudoalcoholic liver injury.
items.reason: This is due to the accumulation of amiodarone and its metabolite in hepatocyte lysosomes and mitochondria.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain the assertion; the pseudoalcoholic liver injury is a metabolic idiosyncrasy.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · AMOXICILLIN-CLAVULANATE HEPATOTOXICITY (IDIOSYNCRATIC MIXED REACTION) | AMIODARONE HEPATOTOXICITY (TOXIC AND IDIOSYNCRATIC REACTION)

items.subtopic: CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND OTHER IMMUNOTHERAPIES FOR CANCER | HIGHLY ACTIVE ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY FOR HIV INFECTION (MITOCHONDRIAL TOXIC, IDIOSYNCRATIC, STEATOSIS; HEPATOCELLULAR, CHOLESTATIC, AND MIXED)
items.assertion: Checkpoint inhibitors can cause hepatitis that responds to glucocorticoid therapy.
items.reason: The hepatitis is associated with activation of the immune response and positive ANAs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Toxic and Drug Induced Hepatitis · CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR AND OTHER IMMUNOTHERAPIES FOR CANCER | HIGHLY ACTIVE ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY FOR HIV INFECTION (MITOCHONDRIAL TOXIC, IDIOSYNCRATIC, STEATOSIS; HEPATOCELLULAR, CHOLESTATIC, AND MIXED)
